FILTERS
ABOUT — Benchmark Methodology & Conclusions [hide]

Methodology

  • Data: 8 Chinese water bodies (4 lakes: West Lake, Taihu, Qinghai, Dongting; 4 rivers: Qiantang, Huangpu, Yangtze-Nanjing, Yangtze-Wuhan)
  • Profiles: 20 real JAXA AW3D30 (~1m/px) + 72 synthetic (parameterized from real statistics)
  • 1D u-axis transects: 512-sample elevation profiles crossing water-land boundaries
  • Ground truth: OSM Overpass API water polygons for real profiles; exact known positions for synthetic
  • Noise models: 3 variants — ideal (σ=0), white noise (σ=0.05m), full terrain-rgb encode/decode chain (0.1m quantization)
  • Algorithm matrix: 52 configurations across 7 dimensions (gradient operator, norm, window stat, pre-smoothing, multi-scale, decision function, flatness)
  • 3-round sweep: independent per-dimension scan → combined optimums → fine flatness tuning

Conclusions

  • Winner: G2 (forward diff) + M4 (squared gradient) + flatness=10-20
  • RMSE: 0.437 (baseline: 0.695, -37%)
  • Boundary offset: 5.1m (baseline: 74.8m, -93%)
  • Flood ratio: 0.087 (baseline: 0.534, -84%)
  • Fragments: 7.4 (baseline: 13.6, -46%)
  • Window statistics (W1-W4): completely ineffective — classify everything as water (RMSE ≈ 0.98)
  • Pre-smoothing (S1/S2): counterproductive — increases boundary offset
  • Multi-scale product (M4): single best dimension, RMSE drops from 0.70 to 0.48 by squaring the gradient
  • G2 forward diff: halves boundary offset vs central diff, with fewer texture fetches (2/axis vs 4)
  • Shader implementation: 3 bicubic fetches (vs 4 in baseline), no sqrt needed, squared gradient amplifies flat↔steep gap
OVERVIEW — Boundary Offset vs Flood Ratio
